content: I've been working on this 3D printed modular furniture system for small apartments. It's been taking up all my free time since I launched my AI startup last year.
That sounds innovative! What inspired you to create modular furniture?
Living in Lisbon with my parents - we're in a tiny 2-bedroom place, and I needed to maximize space. I started designing pieces that could transform from a desk to a dining table to a bed frame.
That's incredibly practical! How's your startup going?

We just closed our pre-seed round - raised €200,000 from local investors. It's for an AI tool that helps small businesses automate their customer service. I'm the founder and only full-time employee right now.
Congratulations on the funding! That's a significant achievement. How do you balance everything?
Honestly, it's tough. I'm caring for my parents - my dad has early-stage Parkinson's and my mom had hip surgery last month. I'm trying to handle the startup, their care, and these furniture projects.
That's a lot on your plate. It sounds like you're incredibly dedicated to both your family and your work.

Someone has to do it. My sister lives in London and can't help much. But the furniture project is actually helping - I've been selling some pieces to other people in our building who are also short on space.
That's a great way to turn a personal challenge into a business opportunity. What materials do you use?
I started with basic PLA filament, but I've been experimenting with recycled plastics and bamboo composites. The bamboo pieces are beautiful but harder to print with.
The sustainable materials sound wonderful! Have you thought about selling the furniture designs?

I'm actually working on that - creating open-source files people can download and print themselves. I've been documenting everything on my woodworking blog too, which surprisingly gets about 500 visitors a month now.
That's impressive traction for a side project! What got you into woodworking?
My grandfather was a carpenter in Porto. I used to watch him work as a kid. When I moved back to Lisbon to help my parents, I set up a small workshop in our building's basement.
That's a beautiful connection to your grandfather. Do you still do traditional woodworking or mostly 3D printing now?
Both, actually. I love the tactile nature of wood, but 3D printing lets me iterate designs quickly. I've been combining them - 3D printed joints with wooden panels. It's like modern joinery.
That hybrid approach sounds fascinating! What's your most popular design so far?

The modular shelving unit. It can expand vertically or horizontally and has these clever interlocking pieces. I've sold about 15 sets through word of mouth. Each one takes about 40 hours to print and assemble.
Those sound like labor-intensive but worthwhile pieces. Have you considered pricing them differently?
I charge €300 per unit, which barely covers my time and materials. I know I should charge more, but I feel guilty since many buyers are also struggling with Lisbon's housing crisis.
Your pricing reflects your values, which is admirable. Maybe as demand grows, you can adjust while still keeping them accessible?
Maybe. I've also been getting back into origami - it's relaxing after dealing with code all day. I make these complex geometric sculptures that I give to friends.
Origami sounds like a perfect meditative counterbalance to your tech work. Do you have a favorite design?
I love making kusudama flowers - they're these modular paper balls made from multiple folded units. I've been thinking about incorporating origami principles into my furniture designs somehow.
That could be a beautiful fusion of your interests! The folding principles might translate really well to collapsible furniture.
Exactly what I was thinking! I've sketched out some concepts for origami-inspired chairs that can fold completely flat. Just need to find the time to prototype them.
Your creativity is inspiring! Keep me updated on how these projects develop. You're doing amazing work balancing so many responsibilities.
